quote.author: Samuel Johnson, kicking a stone, as recorded in James Boswell's Life of Johnson (1791)

macro.title: What It Means — VWRA, Global Bonds, Gold & BTC

reads.label: VWRA
reads.text: pushed by two forces at once — discounted war premium plus an unrelated chip re-rating.

reads.label: Bonds
reads.text: last issue's relief is NOT confirmed; Japan's long end is contested, a live correlation risk.

reads.label: Gold
reads.text: a floor test, not a breakout.

reads.label: BTC
reads.text: through $64k on borrowed risk appetite, not its own story.

stories.kicker: The Good Life · Under the hammer, tomorrow
stories.headline: A Rainbow, Once, Anywhere
stories.dek: Australia's largest-ever Swiss-watch consignment opens for bidding tomorrow, led by a Daytona reference that has never before been offered at auction — anywhere in the world.

stories.headline: The Shark Nobody Had Seen Alive
stories.dek: A "living fossil" older than most of the seafloor it swims over has finally been filmed breathing — not once, but twice, five years and 700 metres apart, in two of the loneliest stretches of the Pacific.

stories.kicker: The World · The same room, a new sign on the door
stories.headline: "Over," and Still Going
stories.dek: Washington says the Hormuz ceasefire is finished. The negotiators are back at the same table in Doha today anyway — a reminder that a declaration and the thing it describes don't always move together.

stories.headline: The Parade Comes Home to the Stadium
stories.dek: After a decade at the Padang, National Day's rehearsal season opens tonight back at the National Stadium — a genuine homecoming for a show that outgrew its old address, and tonight's fireworks are free to watch.

stories.kicker: Property · The Q2 flash, read properly
stories.headline: One Market, Splitting in Two
stories.dek: The headline number says the market is cooling. The composition underneath says something more interesting: prime and landed are reheating while the mass market pulls back — which means where you own now matters more than the average ever did.

stories.headline: The Market Just Agreed With the Hype
stories.dek: SK hynix's Nasdaq debut wasn't just oversubscribed on paper — it closed up 13% on real trading, confirming the largest-ever US listing by a foreign company. The memory supercycle stopped being a story and became a print.

stories.kicker: The Kit · Pack for the flight
stories.headline: A Cinema That Folds Into a Case
stories.dek: Xreal's cheapest glasses yet got their first reviews this week — a $299, 62-gram pair of AR glasses that turn a phone or laptop into a 147-inch virtual screen. For a reader who flies to Tokyo more than most, that's not a gimmick, it's carry-on gear.

stories.kicker: Connected Home · The migrator's weekend project
stories.headline: Hue Finally Speaks Thread — Almost on Its Own
stories.dek: A firmware update lets existing Hue bulbs talk Zigbee and Matter-over-Thread at once. It's real local-first progress for a Lutron migrant — with one honest asterisk: you still need someone else's box in the loop.

stories.headline: Reviews Weren't Lying, for Once
stories.dek: Last issue asked whether Moana's worst-reviewed-remake reception would show up at the till. Thursday's soft previews and a sliding weekend forecast say yes — while a scrappy horror sequel quietly tracks toward a franchise record.

stories.kicker: The Family Desk · Who is she telling?
stories.headline: A Bill, a Number, and a Better Question
stories.dek: A new federal bill wants chatbots to disclose themselves and stop harvesting children's data. The evidence behind it is real — and so is the reminder that the number driving the panic is mostly about teenagers who aren't eleven yet.

stories.kicker: The Macro Desk · Four instruments, one reading
stories.headline: A Correction, on Ourselves
stories.dek: Last issue read Tokyo's long bond as finally breathing easier. Fresher reporting says the opposite — Japan's 10-year is testing a fresh three-decade high, not falling from one. We're printing the conflict, not picking the flattering number.

stories.headline: You Don't Need a Twin, You Need a Fan
stories.dek: A new study of nearly 1,500 friends politely demolishes the "birds of a feather" theory of bonding. What predicts a happy friendship isn't how alike you actually are — it's how generously you see each other.

stories.headline: Lift Heavy Through the Change
stories.dek: A small Canadian trial puts real numbers behind an idea gym-goers have long suspected: heavy, supervised strength work through perimenopause doesn't just preserve muscle and bone — it's the waitlist group that quietly loses ground.

stories.headline: SIA Goes Back to Madrid
stories.dek: After 22 years away, Singapore Airlines returns to the Spanish capital this October — one line in a European build-out that keeps thickening, while at home a decades-old tax finally goes live.
